%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.1-1 A Historical Note

> **Section Objective:** Understand why the number system expanded step by step, and why complex numbers are legitimate tools — not mathematical tricks.

---

Here is a surprising fact: fractions, negative numbers, and complex numbers were all considered strange or suspicious when they first appeared. Each one was accepted only after mathematicians realized that the older number system simply could not handle certain important problems.

This section tells that story in brief. You will see the sequence of expansions — from natural numbers through fractions, irrational numbers, and negative numbers, all the way to complex numbers — and understand why each step was forced by necessity.

You will also see why engineers use \(j = \sqrt{-1}\) and how complex numbers can simplify problems whose starting point and final answer are both real.

#### Exam Note
This page is not about memorizing dates. The tested idea is that complex numbers are legitimate tools introduced to solve problems that real numbers alone cannot handle.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The number system expanded in five stages, each forced by a problem the previous stage could not solve.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777232019886-4251.png)

## 1. Why the Number System Had to Grow

The history of numbers is a history of necessity. Each new type of number was not invented for elegance — it was accepted because the existing system hit a wall.

**Natural numbers** handle counting. But if you need to split a loaf of bread into three equal parts, you need fractions. If you draw a square with side length 1 and ask how long the diagonal is, fractions are not enough: the answer is \(\sqrt{2}\), which cannot be written as any ratio of whole numbers. This forced the acceptance of **irrational numbers**.

Negative numbers came next. The equation \(x + 5 = 0\) has no solution in the natural numbers or fractions — you need \(x = -5\). Once negative numbers were accepted, algebra became far more powerful.

The pattern is always the same: a concrete problem demands a new kind of number, and eventually the mathematical community accepts it.

### EXAM TIP

When a textbook gives history here, the tested idea is almost always: **new numbers are introduced to solve previously impossible expressions or equations.** Recognize the pattern, not the dates.

$$j^2 = -1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sqrt{-1} = \pm j \quad \text{and} \quad \sqrt{-4} = \pm 2j$$
*Engineers write \(j\) instead of \(i\) for \(\sqrt{-1}\) to avoid confusion with \(i\), the standard symbol for electrical current. Once \(j\) is defined by \(j^2 = -1\), any square root of a negative number can be written systematically: for example, \(\sqrt{-4} = \sqrt{4} \cdot \sqrt{-1} = \pm 2j\).*

## 2. How Imaginary Numbers Became Useful

Consider the equation \(x^2 + 1 = 0\). Rearranging gives \(x^2 = -1\). No real number satisfies this, because squaring any real number gives a non-negative result. The real number system simply has no solution.

The fix is to **define** a new number \(j\) such that \(j^2 = -1\). This is not a guess — it is a deliberate extension of the number system, exactly like accepting negative numbers to solve \(x + 5 = 0\).

The historical turning point came with Cardano's work on cubic equations. His formula for \(x^3 - 15x - 4 = 0\) produced intermediate expressions involving \(\sqrt{-121}\). These looked meaningless at the time. Yet when Bombelli followed the algebra carefully, the imaginary parts cancelled and the final answer was the real number \(4\).

This is the key pattern to remember:

> **Real problem \(\to\) complex intermediate step \(\to\) real answer.**

Complex numbers appeared in the middle of the work even though the original equation and its solution were entirely real. This is not a coincidence — it is one of the main reasons complex numbers are useful in engineering mathematics.

### KEY INSIGHT

The presence of \(\sqrt{-121}\) in the working did not mean the problem was unsolvable. It meant the algebra needed a broader number system to pass through.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![unknown](/figures/page-004-unknown-1.png)
*The route through Country Y models how complex numbers can serve as an efficient intermediate path even when both the starting point and the destination are real.*

## 3. Why Mathematicians Finally Accepted Complex Numbers

For a long time, complex numbers were tolerated as an algebraic trick but not trusted as genuine numbers. The shift came largely through the work of **Carl Friedrich Gauss**.

In 1799, Gauss proved the **Fundamental Theorem of Algebra**: every polynomial equation of degree \(n\) has exactly \(n\) roots, provided complex numbers are allowed. This result gave complex numbers a central and unavoidable role in mathematics.

Gauss also helped establish the **complex plane** interpretation: a complex number \(z = a + jb\) is simply a point with coordinates \((a, b)\). This geometric picture removed the mystery. Complex numbers were no longer a strange algebraic symbol — they were points in a two-dimensional plane.

For engineering, this matters directly: representing \(z = a + jb\) as a point is the foundation for everything that follows in this appendix.

### EXAM TIP

History matters here mainly because it explains **legitimacy and motivation** for the algebra that follows — not because exam questions will ask for dates.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Number systems expand when older systems cannot solve important problems — each extension was forced by necessity.
- Complex numbers can simplify real problems: a real equation may pass through complex intermediate steps and return a real answer.
- Gauss's geometric interpretation of the complex plane gave complex numbers full mathematical legitimacy.

*In the next section we will represent complex numbers algebraically and graphically.*
